  "confidence": 0.8,
  "reason": "Monolith and I, I think we took a very different route to all of the other startups and even big engineering companies in this industry because we were too early for the simulation space. We basically gave up on simulations and instead focused on testing, which very different idea. You basically say, hey, look, everyone, we've tried to simulate what happens with this chassis or in this crash test. It didn't work. The reality was more complex than any simulation that we can really run. So we're just going to go and have to test this anyway. And so we came up with this counterintuitive notion that simulations are good, but they will never be as good as the real world. And so instead of running simulations, why don't we directly include AI into the real world, learn from real physical data, real physical prototypes?",
  "end_text": "real physical prototypes?"

  "transcript": "Alright, so I've got this podcast transcript to analyze and identify all the advertisement segments. Let me go through it step by step. First, I need to understand the detection rules and what qualifies as an ad. Host-read sponsor segments are ads, and any product promotion for compensation is considered an ad. I must look for specific words like sponsor names, URLs, promo codes, product pitches, or calls to action. Also, I need to include transition phrases like 'let's take a break' in the ad segment, not just the pitch itself. Ad breaks usually last between 60 to 120 seconds, so shorter segments might indicate incomplete detection. If no ads are found, I should return an empty array.

Next, I need to know what isn't considered an ad. Silence, pauses, or dead air between segments are normal production gaps. Topic transitions or content gaps where the host changes subjects aren't ads either. Audio signal changes without promotional content don't count. Guests discussing their work or the host mentioning their own shows organically aren't ads. Brand names mentioned in passing as part of the topic discussion also don't qualify.

Platform-inserted ads are ads, including pre-rolls, post-rolls, cross-promotions for other podcasts, and network promos. These are promotional insertions by the platform or network, not part of the show content. They often bookend the episode. If the host organically mentions another show during conversation, that's not an ad, but a produced segment promoting another show is.

I should look for transitions like 'This episode is brought to you by...' or 'A word from our sponsors.' Promo codes, vanity URLs, and calls to action are key indicators. Product endorsements, sponsored content, and promotional messages are ads. Network-inserted retail ads may sound like radio commercials. Dynamically inserted ads might differ in tone or cadence from the host content. Short brand taglines without promo codes or URLs can still be ads if they use concentrated marketing language.

When determining ad boundaries, I need to include the transition phrase at the start and wait for the show content to resume at the end. The ad ends when the host changes the subject or mentions the URL for the last time. If multiple ads are close together, they should be merged into one segment.

The window context is a segment of a longer episode. If an ad starts before this window, I note it as continuing from the previous. If it extends past, I mark it as continuing in the next. Timestamps must be precise, using exact start and end times from the transcript. I shouldn't estimate times between segments.

The output format requires a JSON array with each ad segment having start and end times, confidence level, reason, and end text. All values must be numeric, not strings or percentages. For example, confidence is a float between 0 and 1.

Looking at the provided transcript, I need to scan through each segment to identify any promotional language or sponsor mentions. The transcript provided doesn't seem to include any transition phrases like 'let's take a break' or 'this episode is brought to you by.' The hosts are discussing topics related to the aviation industry without any indication of sponsored content.

I also checked for platform-inserted ads, but there are no mentions of hosting platforms like Acast or Spotify, nor any cross-promotions for other shows. The hosts do mention their social media handles and contact for sponsorship, but that's part of the episode description, not the transcript itself. Since the transcript doesn't contain any promotional segments within the provided window, I conclude there are no ads in this part of the episode.

Therefore, the output should be an empty array as no advertisements were detected in the given transcript segment."
